metadata.name: NZ->UK Dairy & Lamb Export Corridor (W4M-WWG) -- Instance Data
metadata.domain: International Dairy & Lamb Trade / Cold Chain Logistics
metadata.description: Complete graph of the NZ dairy (butter, cheese, milk powder) and lamb (chilled/frozen) corridor from Christchurch processing through Lyttelton to Panama Canal or Suez to Felixstowe/London Gateway and UK distribution. Includes 10 chain nodes, 8 shipment legs, 10 parties, 7 compliance gates, 12 trade documents, 8 bottlenecks with risk assessments and net impact, 4 hypotheses, 3 scenarios, 5 cascade effects, 3 predictive patterns, 1 example shipment.

examples.name: NZ Dairy & Lamb -> UK Retail & Wholesale
examples.description: Chilled NZ lamb and frozen/chilled dairy products (butter, cheese, milk powder) from MPI-accredited processing in Canterbury to UK distribution via Port of Lyttelton and Panama Canal (primary) or Suez via Singapore (alternative). NZ lamb: 60-day shelf life from slaughter, 35-42 day transit, leaving 18-25 days for UK retail. Dairy products: 6-12 month shelf life (cheese/butter), shorter for fresh milk powder. NZ-UK FTA 2023 preferential tariff. CIF Felixstowe terms.
examples.commodity: NZ dairy (butter, cheese, milk powder) and lamb (chilled/frozen)
examples.regulatoryRegime: NZ-UK FTA 2023; NZ Animal Products Act 1999; UK BTOM 2024; MPI Export Requirements

examples.name: Christchurch Processing Plant
examples.capabilities: slaughter, boning, vacuum-pack, blast-chill, blast-freeze, MPI-accredited, halal-certified, dairy processing co-location

examples.name: Lyttelton Cold Storage (Pre-Haulage Staging)
examples.operator: Lyttelton Port Company / Hilton Foods NZ
examples.capabilities: -1C to -25C holding, container pre-loading, reefer pre-cool, MPI inspection staging, multi-commodity separation

examples.name: Port of Lyttelton -- Reefer Terminal
examples.capabilities: reefer-plug, container-yard, MPI-clearance, customs-clearance, weighbridge, seismic-resilient infrastructure

examples.name: Panama Canal Transit
examples.location: Panama Canal -- Neopanamax Locks (Agua Clara / Cocoli)

examples.name: Felixstowe Border Control Post (BCP)
examples.operator: APHA (Animal and Plant Health Agency) / Port Health Authority

examples.name: London Gateway Alternative Port
examples.capabilities: reefer-plug, customs-clearance, BCP-facility, container-yard, rail-connection, overflow-capacity

examples.name: Daventry WWG Cold Store
examples.location: DIRFT (Daventry International Rail Freight Terminal), Northamptonshire
examples.capabilities: -1C to -25C holding, multi-customer, pick-and-pack, retail-ready-packaging, stock-rotation, dairy-segregation

examples.name: Regional Distribution Hub
examples.location: Various (Midlands, North West, South East regional DCs)

examples.name: Customer Delivery Point
examples.location: Various (UK supermarket DCs, foodservice distributors, wholesale markets)

examples.route: Lyttelton then Tasman Sea then South Pacific then Panama Canal approach
Panama Canal then Caribbean Sea then North Atlantic then English Channel then Felixstowe

examples.name: Silver Fern Farms Ltd
examples.role: NZ meat and dairy processor and exporter -- slaughter, dairy processing, packing, export documentation
examples.responsibilities: Product quality, MPI compliance, health certificate application, container loading, shelf-life guarantee, Animal Products Act compliance
examples.riskOwnership: Product quality to FOB. Cold chain to FCA Christchurch. Regulatory compliance (NZ side).
examples.licences: MPI Registered Exporter RMP-4721, MPI Risk Management Programme, Halal Certification HC-NZ-0312

examples.name: World Wide Group (WWG) UK Ltd
examples.role: UK importer of record -- customs clearance, IPAFFS notification, BCP liaison, cold storage, UK distribution
examples.responsibilities: UK import compliance, IPAFFS pre-notification, CHED-P submission, duty payment, cold storage management, retailer and foodservice delivery
examples.riskOwnership: All risk from CIF Felixstowe. UK regulatory compliance. Storage and distribution costs.
examples.licences: HMRC EORI GB-741293856000, IPAFFS Registered Importer, DEFRA Approved Food Business Operator

examples.name: Kotahi Logistics NZ
examples.role: Origin freight forwarder -- booking, documentation coordination, container logistics, MPI liaison
examples.responsibilities: Carrier booking, container pre-cool verification, export customs lodgement, B/L instruction, insurance arrangement, MPI coordination
examples.riskOwnership: Operational coordination. No cargo risk (agent only).

examples.name: Maersk Line A/S
examples.role: Ocean carrier -- Lyttelton to Felixstowe via Panama Canal (primary) or Suez via Singapore (alternative)
examples.responsibilities: Vessel operation, reefer container supply, temperature monitoring at sea, on-time arrival, B/L issuance, Panama Canal transit scheduling
examples.riskOwnership: Carrier liability under Hague-Visby Rules (SDR 2 per kg or SDR 666.67 per package). Reefer machinery breakdown.

examples.name: Customs Connect NZ Ltd
examples.role: NZ customs broker -- export declaration, tariff classification, NZ-UK FTA origin certification
examples.responsibilities: Export declaration lodgement (NZCS), NZ-UK FTA Certificate of Origin preparation, duty drawback claims
examples.riskOwnership: Declaration accuracy. Penalty liability for misclassification.

examples.name: Clearfreight UK Ltd
examples.role: UK customs broker -- import declaration, BTOM compliance, duty/VAT, NZ-UK FTA preference claims
examples.responsibilities: Import customs declaration (CDS), tariff classification, NZ-UK FTA preference claim, duty payment, BTOM compliance

examples.name: MPI (Ministry for Primary Industries)
examples.role: NZ export regulatory authority -- meat and dairy inspection, health certification, establishment audit, Animal Products Act enforcement
examples.responsibilities: Ante/post-mortem inspection, health certificate issuance, export eligibility, establishment registration, residue testing, dairy standard verification
examples.riskOwnership: Regulatory authority -- sets the rules. Non-negotiable.
examples.contactSLA: Government SLA (2-5 business days standard, 24 hours urgent)

examples.name: APHA (Animal & Plant Health Agency)
examples.role: UK border SPS authority -- import inspection, health certificate verification, physical checks for NZ animal products
examples.responsibilities: CHED-P verification, identity checks, physical inspection (random and risk-based), laboratory sampling, import authorisation
examples.riskOwnership: Regulatory authority -- can reject, detain, or destroy non-compliant consignments.

examples.name: Mainfreight NZ (Refrigerated Division)
examples.role: Domestic reefer haulage -- Christchurch processing to Port of Lyttelton
examples.responsibilities: Reefer truck operation, temperature maintenance, GPS tracking, timely delivery to port cut-off

examples.name: Reed Boardall Transport Ltd
examples.role: UK inland reefer haulage -- Felixstowe/London Gateway to Daventry and onwards
examples.responsibilities: Container collection from BCP, reefer temperature maintenance, delivery to cold store, empty container return
examples.riskOwnership: In-transit temperature UK side. Delay to cold store.

examples.name: Lineage Logistics UK
examples.role: UK cold storage and distribution -- stockholding, pick-pack, retailer delivery prep, dairy and lamb segregation
examples.responsibilities: Temperature-controlled storage, stock rotation (FIFO), date-code management, retail-ready packaging, order fulfilment, dairy/lamb segregation compliance
examples.riskOwnership: Storage temperature integrity. Stock loss through date expiry.

examples.name: MPI Health Certificate & Export Eligibility
examples.commonFailureReasons: Residue test positive (MRL breach), document mismatch between health cert and commercial invoice, RMP non-conformance, dairy compositional standard failure

examples.name: NZ Export Customs Clearance
examples.commonFailureReasons: Tariff classification dispute, incomplete export declaration, sanctions screening flag

examples.name: IPAFFS Pre-Notification (UK)
examples.avgDuration: Must be submitted 1-5 days before arrival
examples.commonFailureReasons: Late submission, health certificate number mismatch, commodity code error (dairy/meat cross-reference), missing CHED-P reference, dual-commodity IPAFFS complexity

examples.name: BCP Document & Identity Check
examples.commonFailureReasons: Health cert / CHED-P mismatch, seal number discrepancy, container markings inconsistent with documentation, dual-commodity declaration errors

examples.name: BCP Physical / SPS Inspection
examples.commonFailureReasons: Temperature excursion evidence, visual contamination, off-odour on lamb, dairy packaging damage, positive lab result (Listeria, Salmonella), butter rancidity

examples.name: UK Import Customs Clearance (CDS)
examples.commonFailureReasons: Incorrect tariff code (dairy vs meat classification), NZ-UK FTA preference claim rejected (origin proof insufficient), duty underpayment, dual-commodity customs entry complexity

examples.name: Cold Chain Temperature Verification
examples.authority: APHA / Port Health (mandatory for all chilled/frozen animal products)
examples.commonFailureReasons: Temperature excursion >5C recorded during 35-42 day voyage, data logger malfunction, gaps in recording during Panama transit, dual-temperature container variance

examples.issuingAuthority: Federation of Islamic Associations of NZ (FIANZ)

examples._section: === BOTTLENECKS (The Tight Spots) with Risk + Net Impact ===

examples.name: Panama Canal Congestion / Drought Restrictions
examples.description: Panama Canal drought restrictions reduce daily transit slots from 36 to 22-24. Queue times extend 5-14 days. Fresh water rationing limits draught to 44 feet (vs 50ft design). NZ-UK via Panama is the primary route -- blockage forces complete reroute via Suez (+7-10 days) or Cape Horn (+15 days).
examples.typicalDelay: 5-14 days (drought queue) or full reroute +7-15 days
examples.seasonality: Worse Jan-May (Panama dry season / El Nino years)
examples.rootCause: Climate-driven freshwater scarcity in Gatun Lake; canal design dependency on rainfall; structural throughput limit
examples.mitigationStrategy: Dual-route pricing in contracts (Panama primary, Suez fallback); advance booking slots 4 weeks; drought surcharge pass-through clauses
examples.workaroundDescription: Reroute via Suez Canal (Singapore transhipment, +7-10 days). Worst case: Cape Horn routing (+15 days, only viable for frozen product).

examples.name: NZ Dairy Seasonality (May-Oct Peak Production)
examples.description: NZ dairy production is pasture-based and highly seasonal: 70% of annual production May-Oct (spring flush). Export volumes spike dramatically. Port of Lyttelton reefer yard reaches 95%+ capacity. Carrier equipment diverted to dairy at expense of lamb bookings.
examples.seasonality: May-Oct peak (NZ spring flush / milking season)
examples.rootCause: Pasture-based dairy system creates extreme seasonal production curve; NZ export-dependent (95% of dairy exported)
examples.mitigationStrategy: Forward booking 8 weeks ahead for peak season; coordinate dairy and lamb shipment windows; multi-port strategy (Auckland overflow)
examples.workaroundDescription: Divert lamb shipments to Auckland/Tauranga during dairy peak; negotiate dedicated reefer allocation with Maersk

examples.name: BCP Inspection Queue & Lab Turnaround
examples.description: UK BTOM physical inspection rates for NZ lamb and dairy run 10-25%. Dual-commodity containers (lamb + dairy) may trigger double inspection. Lab sample turnaround 2-5 days. BCP has limited reefer holding. Shared bottleneck with AU corridor.
examples.rootCause: BTOM inspection regime; limited BCP capacity; dual-commodity complexity; lab resource constraints
examples.mitigationStrategy: Pre-arrival documentation quality checks; separate dairy and lamb containers to simplify inspection; maintain trusted trader status; avoid Monday/Friday arrivals

examples.name: UK Cold Storage Capacity Shortage
examples.description: UK chilled/frozen storage operating at 92-96% capacity nationally. Christmas hits 99%+. NZ dairy requires separate storage zones (allergen and cross-contamination compliance). When tight, rates spike 30-50% and consignments redirect. Shared bottleneck with AU corridor.
examples.typicalDelay: 1-3 days (finding capacity) plus additional transport if redirected
examples.rootCause: Structural underinvestment in UK cold chain infrastructure; energy cost inflation; dairy segregation requirements reduce effective capacity
examples.mitigationStrategy: 12-month storage contract with guaranteed dairy allocation; pre-book 8 weeks for peak; maintain secondary facility; dedicated dairy zone
examples.workaroundDescription: Redirect to secondary cold store (Grimsby/Avonmouth) at +15% transport cost; short-term reefer storage at port (max 5 days)

examples.name: Shelf Life Pressure (35-42 Day Transit)
examples.description: NZ-UK is the longest major chilled meat corridor globally (19,000 km). 35-42 day transit consumes 58-70% of lamb shelf life (60 days). Any delay pushes remaining life below UK retailer 14-day minimum. Dairy is less affected (6-12 month shelf life for butter/cheese) but fresh milk powder has tighter windows.
examples.typicalDelay: No delay -- but structural shelf-life consumption from distance
examples.seasonality: Worse when Panama delays add 5-14 days
examples.rootCause: Geographic distance (19,000 km Lyttelton to Felixstowe); no viable shorter route; lamb shelf life limited to 60 days chilled
examples.mitigationStrategy: Process lamb within 24 hours of slaughter; blast-chill to -1.5C (extend to 70-day shelf life); dual-temperature containers (lamb chilled, dairy frozen); consider frozen-only for lamb during Panama restrictions
examples.workaroundDescription: Switch lamb to frozen (extends to 24 months) at 15-20% price discount; dairy unaffected

examples.name: NZ Port Labour Disruptions
examples.description: NZ port workers (MUNZ) have history of industrial action. Lyttelton experienced 3-week disruption in 2022. Container handling rates drop 40-60% during go-slow action. Complete stoppage forces diversion to Tauranga (+1 day road, +2 days port queue).
examples.seasonality: Unpredictable -- typically during contract negotiation years (every 3 years)
examples.rootCause: Collective agreement cycles; port automation disputes; container terminal privatisation tensions
examples.mitigationStrategy: Dual-port strategy (Lyttelton primary, Auckland secondary); monitor MUNZ negotiation status; maintain 2-week buffer stock in UK
examples.workaroundDescription: Divert to Port of Auckland or Tauranga. Road transfer Christchurch to Auckland adds 1 day and NZD 3,500.

examples.name: Extreme Weather (Southern Ocean / Tasman Sea)
examples.description: Southern Ocean routing from NZ encounters some of the world's roughest seas. Tasman Sea storms Oct-Apr cause vessel delays and schedule disruption. Container lashing failures risk reefer power disconnection. Cyclone season (Jan-Mar) adds variability.
examples.seasonality: Oct-Apr (Southern Hemisphere storm season), Jan-Mar (tropical cyclone risk)
examples.rootCause: Geographic exposure to Southern Ocean weather systems; Tasman Sea fetch; vessel routing through storm corridors
examples.mitigationStrategy: Weather routing services (DTN/Weathernews); carrier schedule buffers; pre-trip container lashing inspection; dual data loggers

examples.name: NZ-UK FTA Documentation Complexity
examples.description: NZ-UK FTA 2023 is new and complex. Dairy tariff rate quotas (TRQs) have annual limits. Lamb quota management requires careful tracking. Self-declaration regime means exporter bears verification burden. HMRC retrospective audits (5 years) can reclaim duties + penalties. Dual commodity (dairy + lamb) compounds tariff classification complexity.
examples.typicalDelay: No delay -- but retrospective duty clawback risk NZD 30,000-120,000 per audit
examples.rootCause: New FTA (effective 2023); self-declaration regime; TRQ management complexity; dual-commodity tariff classification; limited NZ exporter FTA experience
examples.mitigationStrategy: Dedicated FTA compliance officer; quarterly origin compliance audits; TRQ utilisation tracking dashboard; annual legal review; exporter training programme

examples.cascadeEffect: Shelf-life erosion on lamb pushes remaining days below 14-day retailer minimum; Panama surcharge NZD 4,000-8,000 per container; multiple containers affected simultaneously
Lamb bookings displaced by dairy volume; equipment premium NZD 2,000-4,000 per container; port congestion surcharge

examples.regulatoryConsequence: Increased future inspection frequency if non-conformance found; dual-commodity triggers additional scrutiny
examples.cascadeEffect: Demurrage accumulates; cold store booking missed; retailer delivery delayed; lamb shelf life critical
Redirected to secondary facility; higher transport; container detained at port GBP 120/day; dairy segregation limits redirect options
Lamb arriving with <14 days shelf life: rejected or 25-40% markdown. Diverted to food service at 35% loss. Dairy unaffected but lamb revenue represents 60% of corridor value.
Diversion to Auckland adds NZD 3,500/container road cost; Auckland port queue adds 2 days; vessel schedule disrupted for 2-4 weeks post-action
Container lashing damage risk; reefer power interruption; schedule delays cascade through carrier network for 1-2 weeks
Retrospective duty clawback NZD 30,000-120,000 per audit; dairy TRQ exhaustion forces MFN rate (35% vs 0%); reputation risk with HMRC

examples.statement: Chilled lamb and dairy shipped Lyttelton to Felixstowe via Panama Canal will arrive within 38 days of dispatch 75% of the time, with 90% arriving within 42 days
examples.baselineValue: Historical average 36 days (2024-2025, n=78, Panama route only)
examples.measurementMethod: Container GPS tracking gate-out Lyttelton to gate-in Felixstowe
examples.dataSource: Maersk container tracking API + Kotahi booking records 2024-2025
examples.variance: -3% on P75, -3% on P90 -- Panama drought restrictions worse than modelled
examples.learningNote: Panama Canal drought adds 3-5 day variability. El Nino years dramatically worse. Suez alternative adds 7-10 days but more predictable schedule.
examples.nextIteration: Model separately: Panama normal (36 days) vs Panama drought (43 days) vs Suez fallback (45 days). Weight by seasonal drought probability.

examples.statement: Chilled NZ lamb will arrive at UK cold store with minimum 18 days remaining shelf life from 60-day product, providing 14+ days for UK retail distribution
examples.baselineValue: 60-day total shelf life (blast-chilled); 2 days processing + 38 days transit = 40 days consumed
examples.predictedValue: 20 days remaining at Daventry DC arrival
examples.measurementMethod: Shelf-life = slaughter date + 60 days - arrival date at Daventry
examples.dataSource: Silver Fern Farms dispatch records + Lineage Logistics UK intake records
examples.actualOutcome: 2025 full year: mean 19 days remaining; 62% had >18 days; 8 shipments <14 days (all Panama-delayed or weather-delayed)
examples.variance: -1 day vs prediction; 8 below retailer threshold -- unacceptably high failure rate
examples.learningNote: NZ-UK is at the absolute limit of chilled lamb viability. Blast-chill to -1.5C extends to 70 days, improving margin to 28+ days. Frozen lamb eliminates risk entirely at cost of 15-20% price discount.
examples.nextIteration: Test blast-chill protocol (-1.5C, 70-day shelf life). If 90%+ achieve >21 days remaining, standardise for all NZ lamb.

examples.statement: NZ dairy seasonal production peak (May-Oct) will cause 40% of lamb shipments to experience 3+ day equipment delay at Lyttelton due to reefer competition
examples.baselineValue: 2024-25 peak: 35% of lamb shipments delayed 3+ days by dairy reefer competition
examples.predictedValue: 40% affected (increasing dairy export volumes to China and UK)
examples.measurementMethod: Days from lamb booking confirmation to container available for pre-cool during dairy peak
examples.dataSource: Kotahi booking records; Maersk equipment dashboard; LPC reefer yard occupancy; MPI dairy export stats
examples.actualOutcome: 2025 peak season: 35% of lamb delayed (lower than predicted due to new Maersk reefer additions)
examples.variance: -5% -- Maersk fleet addition offset dairy growth
examples.learningNote: Dairy vs lamb reefer competition is structural. Fonterra has priority allocation agreements. Lamb exporters are second-tier for equipment.
examples.nextIteration: Model Fonterra allocation share; negotiate dedicated lamb reefer pool with alternative carrier (CMA CGM)

examples.statement: Maersk schedule reliability on the NZ-UK Panama route will remain below 70% through 2026 due to Panama Canal congestion, with Suez alternative achieving 75% reliability
examples.measurementMethod: Vessel arrival vs scheduled arrival at Felixstowe (within +/- 1 day)
examples.dataSource: Sea-Intelligence carrier reliability data; Maersk published schedules vs actuals; Drewry Maritime
examples.learningNote: Panama reliability is weather-dependent (drought). Suez reliability is geopolitics-dependent (Red Sea). Neither route offers >80% certainty. Dual-route flexibility is the only robust strategy.

examples.name: Panama Canal Closure / Severe Drought Restriction
examples.triggerCondition: Panama Canal restricts daily transits to <18 slots for >30 days OR full closure
examples.assumptions: Suez routing available via Singapore; no additional chokepoint conflict; carrier capacity maintained; El Nino forecast active
examples.transitTimeChange: +7-10 days (Suez reroute) or +15 days (Cape Horn -- frozen only)
examples.shelfLifeImpact: Remaining lamb shelf life drops to 8-13 days -- below 14-day retailer minimum. Must switch to frozen.
examples.mitigationPlan: Switch all lamb to frozen (-20% price); dairy unaffected by time but cost increase; activate Suez pricing; negotiate Panama drought contract clauses
examples.predictiveSignals: Gatun Lake levels below 79 feet; ACP transit restriction announcements; El Nino Southern Oscillation Index; canal waiting time >7 days

examples.name: NZ Dairy Export Ban from Disease Outbreak
examples.triggerCondition: Mycoplasma bovis or foot-and-mouth detected in NZ dairy herd; MPI suspends all dairy export certificates
examples.assumptions: Lamb unaffected (separate certification); MPI rapid response; UK demand redirects to EU/AU dairy
examples.transitTimeChange: Complete halt to dairy exports; lamb may continue
examples.costChange: Total dairy revenue loss NZD 3.2M annually for WWG corridor
examples.mitigationPlan: Pivot to lamb-only corridor; source dairy from AU (Bega/Fonterra AU); maintain NZ-UK FTA capacity for rapid restart; insurance (export credit)
examples.predictiveSignals: MPI disease surveillance bulletins; Mycoplasma bovis eradication programme status; biosecurity incident reports; OIE notifications

examples.name: Christmas Demand Spike -- Lamb & Dairy Collision
examples.triggerCondition: Nov-Dec with UK cold storage >97% AND retail lamb demand +30% AND NZ dairy spring flush in pipeline AND Felixstowe >90% berth occupancy
examples.assumptions: Normal weather; no industrial action; NZ dairy peak finishing; UK lamb demand high for Christmas roasts
examples.costChange: +35-55% cold storage; +20% UK haulage; +15% demurrage risk; dairy storage premium due to segregation
examples.capacityChange: -35% available cold storage at Daventry (dairy + lamb + AU beef competing)
examples.shelfLifeImpact: 2-4 days additional consumed on lamb -- critical at these margins
examples.mitigationPlan: Pre-position 4 weeks lamb buffer by end-Oct; book cold store dairy zone by Aug; secondary at Grimsby; coordinate with AU beef importer to stagger arrivals; Tuesday-Thursday arrival windows
examples.predictiveSignals: Cold store >93% by mid-Oct; Felixstowe berth >85%; retailer Christmas lamb orders up >25%; NZ dairy spring flush volume forecasts

examples.name: Transit Length Shelf-Life Erosion leads to Lamb Rejection / Markdown
examples.description: NZ-UK is the longest chilled meat corridor. 35-42 day transit consumes 58-70% of 60-day lamb shelf life. ANY additional delay (Panama, weather, BCP) pushes remaining days below UK retailer 14-day minimum. Below 10 days: unsaleable, diverted to pet food at 60% loss. This is the corridor's defining structural risk.
examples.triggerDelay: Immediate -- every day of delay erodes shelf life by one day, starting from already-tight margins
examples.affectedParties: Importer (bears rejection cost); exporter (reputational, future order risk); retailer (stock-out if rejected)
examples.preventionAction: Blast-chill to -1.5C for 70-day shelf life; switch to frozen for Panama drought periods; maintain minimum 22-day shelf life at dispatch gate

examples.name: NZ Dairy Disruption leads to UK Substitution Pressure
examples.description: If NZ dairy supply disrupted (disease ban, Panama closure, seasonality gap), UK buyers rapidly substitute EU dairy (Ireland, Netherlands, France). NZ dairy has no unique product advantage (commodity market). Once lost, NZ dairy shelf space takes 6-12 months to recover through re-listing. UK retailers maintain 3+ approved dairy suppliers.
examples.triggerDelay: 2-4 weeks (buyer switches within one order cycle)
examples.affectedParties: NZ exporter (lost revenue, stranded production); WWG importer (margin loss on alternative sourcing); UK retailer (temporary price increase)
examples.preventionAction: Maintain 6-week UK buffer stock; diversify dairy sourcing (AU Fonterra as backup); long-term retailer contracts with volume commitments

examples.name: Cold Storage Overflow leads to Container Detention at Port
examples.description: When Daventry DC full (NZ dairy + lamb + AU beef all competing), containers cannot be stripped. Dairy requires segregated zones, limiting overflow options. Containers sit at Felixstowe/London Gateway. Detention GBP 85/day from day 5. Reefer plug scarcity at port during peak. NZ and AU corridors compound each other.
examples.triggerDelay: 3-5 days after cold store reaches capacity
examples.affectedParties: Importer (detention); port (yard congestion); shipping line (equipment off-hire); cold store (overflow management)
examples.preventionAction: Secondary cold store pre-arranged with dairy capability; stagger NZ and AU arrivals; pre-Christmas stock build from October; dedicated dairy overflow at Thurrock

examples.name: Repeated Delivery Failures leads to Contract Penalties & De-listing
examples.description: UK supermarket and foodservice buyers operate strict KPIs: OTIF >95%, shelf-life compliance >98%. NZ corridor's structural shelf-life risk means failure rate is higher than AU (8% vs 3%). After 3+ failures in 6 months, buyer imposes penalty (2-5% of invoice) then triggers supplier review. De-listing takes 12-18 months to reverse.
examples.affectedParties: Exporter (penalties then lost revenue); importer (margin compression then lost account); retailer (re-sourcing cost)
examples.preventionAction: Dedicated NZ corridor KPI dashboard; proactive delay notification 48 hours ahead; UK buffer stock; frozen fallback for at-risk shipments; quarterly performance review with retailers

examples.name: Claims History leads to Insurance Premium Increase and Coverage Restrictions
examples.description: NZ-UK corridor's longer transit and shelf-life risk generate higher claims frequency than shorter corridors. Marine cargo premiums increase 15-30% following total-loss claim. After 2+ claims in 12 months, insurers may impose deductible increases, exclude chilled lamb, or decline renewal. NZD 10,000-20,000 additional annually.
examples.affectedParties: Exporter (premium payer); importer (CIF cost increase); insurer (claims ratio deterioration)
examples.preventionAction: Risk management programme; blast-chill investment; real-time reefer monitoring; multi-insurer panel; frozen fallback reduces claims frequency

examples.name: NZ Dairy Spring Flush Cycle (May-Oct)
examples.description: NZ dairy production follows a dramatic seasonal curve: 70% of annual production concentrated in May-Oct (spring/summer milking season). Export volumes spike. Lyttelton reefer yard reaches 95%+ capacity Jul-Sep. Fonterra and other dairy co-ops have priority equipment allocation. Lamb exporters are squeezed on reefer availability and port slots.
examples.leadIndicators: Fonterra forecast milk production (published March); LPC reefer yard occupancy >85% by May; Maersk equipment utilisation dashboard; NZ dairy auction (GDT) price signal
examples.typicalImpact: 3-7 day equipment wait for lamb; 15-20% of lamb shipments require alternative port; NZD 2,000-4,000 equipment premium per container
examples.recommendedAction: Book lamb reefer equipment 8 weeks ahead for May-Oct; negotiate dedicated allocation outside Fonterra pool; consider Auckland port for peak months

examples.name: Southern Ocean / Tasman Sea Storm Season (Oct-Apr)
examples.description: Southern Ocean and Tasman Sea generate severe weather systems Oct-Apr. Vessel delays 1-4 days per event, 4-6 events per season. Container lashing failures during heavy weather can disconnect reefer power. Post-storm port congestion adds 2-5 days. NZ is uniquely exposed (more than AU) due to Southern Ocean routing.
examples.leadIndicators: MetService NZ severe weather warnings; NIWA seasonal outlook; vessel schedule delays >24 hours; carrier weather advisory notices
examples.typicalImpact: 1-4 day vessel delay; 2-5 day post-storm port congestion; reefer power interruption risk; lamb shelf-life erosion
examples.recommendedAction: Monitor MetService 5-day forecast; weather routing service (DTN); schedule buffer 3 days for Oct-Apr sailings; dual data loggers; pre-trip container lashing inspection

examples.name: NZ-UK FTA Processing Time Cycle
examples.description: NZ-UK FTA (effective May 2023) is still maturing. Customs processing times for NZ-origin preference claims have decreased 40% since launch but remain 2x longer than established EU/AU trade agreements. Dairy TRQ management creates end-of-year quota rush (Oct-Dec) with processing bottlenecks. HMRC audit frequency declining as compliance database builds.
examples.observedFrequency: Quarterly cycle aligned with TRQ periods
examples.leadIndicators: HMRC TRQ utilisation data (published quarterly); NZ Trade & Enterprise FTA uptake statistics; HMRC audit notice frequency; customs processing time reports
examples.typicalImpact: 1-3 day customs processing delay for audited shipments; NZD 5,000-15,000 per TRQ classification error; dairy TRQ exhaustion forces MFN rate
examples.recommendedAction: Track TRQ utilisation monthly; submit dairy under TRQ early in quota year (Jan-Mar); pre-clearance documentation review; annual FTA compliance training; build HMRC trusted trader relationship

examples.commodity: Chilled NZ lamb -- leg, shoulder, rack (vacuum-packed primals) with 4 pallets NZ butter
examples.quantity: 18 tonnes lamb + 6 tonnes butter (1 x 40ft HC reefer, dual-temperature partition)
